questions.q: In which year was the Kesavananda Bharati case decided?
questions.explanation: The Kesavananda Bharati case was decided in 1973.

questions.q: The Kesavananda Bharati case is most famous for establishing which doctrine?
questions.explanation: The case established the basic structure doctrine.

questions.q: According to the Kesavananda Bharati ruling, Parliament cannot amend which part of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Court held that Parliament cannot amend the basic structure of the Constitution.

questions.q: Which court delivered the judgment in the Kesavananda Bharati case?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India delivered the judgment.

questions.q: The Kesavananda Bharati case was decided by the largest constitutional bench in the history of which institution?
questions.explanation: It was decided by the largest constitutional bench in the history of the Supreme Court of India.

questions.q: The Kesavananda Bharati case concerned the limits of which power of Parliament?
options: The power to amend the Constitution
questions.explanation: The case concerned the scope of Parliament's power to amend the Constitution.

questions.q: The Kesavananda Bharati case is classified in this deck as which type of entry?
questions.explanation: The case is presented as a landmark event in India's constitutional history.

questions.q: The 'basic structure' that the case protects refers to the fundamental framework of the:
questions.explanation: The basic structure doctrine protects the fundamental framework of the Constitution.

questions.q: The Kesavananda Bharati judgment was delivered during which decade?
questions.explanation: The judgment was delivered in 1973, during the 1970s.

questions.q: The outcome of the Kesavananda Bharati case placed a limit on the amending power held by which body?
questions.explanation: The judgment limited Parliament's power to amend the Constitution.

questions.q: How many judges sat on the constitutional bench that decided the Kesavananda Bharati case?
questions.explanation: A bench of thirteen judges, the largest in the Supreme Court's history, decided the case.

questions.q: Who was the Chief Justice of India presiding over the Kesavananda Bharati bench?
questions.explanation: Chief Justice S.M. Sikri presided over the thirteen-judge bench in the Kesavananda Bharati case.

questions.q: The Kesavananda Bharati doctrine acts as a check that aligns with which broader judicial power of the higher courts?
questions.explanation: The basic structure doctrine is exercised through judicial review, by which courts test the validity of laws and amendments.

questions.q: The petitioner who gave his name to the case, Kesavananda Bharati, was the head of a religious establishment in which state?
questions.explanation: Kesavananda Bharati was the head of a mutt (religious institution) in Kerala.

questions.q: The provision of the Constitution that lays down the procedure for amendment, central to the Kesavananda Bharati case, is:
questions.explanation: Article 368 governs the amendment procedure, whose limits the case examined.

questions.q: The Kesavananda Bharati ruling held that while Parliament can amend the Constitution, it cannot do what to its basic structure?
questions.explanation: The Court held Parliament may amend the Constitution but cannot damage or destroy its basic structure.

questions.q: The Kesavananda Bharati case is widely regarded as a landmark in defining the balance between which two organs of the State?
questions.explanation: The case shaped the balance between Parliament's amending power and the judiciary's review power.

questions.q: Several later principles, such as the rule of law and separation of powers, have been recognised by courts as part of which concept from this case?
questions.explanation: Courts have identified principles like the rule of law and separation of powers as part of the basic structure.

questions.q: The Kesavananda Bharati decision is significant because it limits amendments even when Parliament uses which kind of majority?
options: A special majority under the amendment procedure
A two-thirds vote of one state
questions.explanation: The doctrine restrains amendments even those passed with the special majority required by Article 368.

questions.q: The basic structure doctrine from this case is often invoked to protect features such as the supremacy of the Constitution and which other principle?
options: The length of Parliament's lunch break
The federal character of the country
questions.explanation: Features like the supremacy of the Constitution and its federal character have been treated as part of the basic structure.

questions.q: The Kesavananda Bharati case partly reconsidered an earlier Supreme Court view on amendments expressed in which 1967 case?
questions.explanation: The case revisited the position taken in the 1967 Golaknath case regarding Parliament's amending power.

questions.q: The bench in Kesavananda Bharati delivered its verdict by which kind of split?
options: An even 6 to 6 with one absent
questions.explanation: The basic structure doctrine emerged from a narrow majority of 7 to 6 among the thirteen judges.

questions.q: The Kesavananda Bharati petition originally challenged state laws relating to which subject affecting the petitioner's institution?
questions.explanation: The petition arose from a challenge to land reform laws that affected the petitioner's religious institution's property.

questions.q: The basic structure doctrine laid down in 1973 was later applied to strike down parts of which amendment in the Minerva Mills case of 1980?
questions.explanation: In Minerva Mills (1980) the Court used the basic structure doctrine to strike down certain provisions of the 42nd Amendment.

questions.q: The full formal name of the dispute is Kesavananda Bharati versus the State of which entity?
questions.explanation: The case is formally titled Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala.

questions.q: The Kesavananda Bharati judgment running to many opinions was one of the longest ever delivered by the Court, reflecting that the bench produced how many separate sets of opinions?
options: Multiple separate opinions across the thirteen judges
questions.explanation: The thirteen judges produced multiple separate opinions, making the judgment one of the longest the Court has issued.

questions.q: Because it caps Parliament's amending power, the Kesavananda Bharati doctrine effectively reinforces which related principle as part of the constitutional order?
options: The supremacy of the Council of Ministers
questions.explanation: By limiting amendments, the doctrine reinforces the supremacy of the Constitution itself.

questions.q: The Kesavananda Bharati case was heard over an extended period, with the verdict pronounced in which month of 1973?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court pronounced its verdict in April 1973 after lengthy hearings.

questions.q: The Kesavananda Bharati doctrine has since been used by courts to review not just amendments but to safeguard which feature when assessing constitutional changes?
options: The essential identity of the Constitution
The seating plan of the House
questions.explanation: The doctrine protects the essential identity or core framework of the Constitution against alteration.

questions.q: Which statement most accurately captures the holding of the Kesavananda Bharati case?
options: Parliament cannot amend the Constitution at all
Parliament can amend the Constitution but not its basic structure
Parliament can amend the Constitution without any limit
Only the President can amend the Constitution
questions.explanation: The case held that Parliament may amend the Constitution but may not alter its basic structure.
